protocol: medical - respiratory distress/asthma/copd/croup/reactive airway (respiratory distress - croup/epiglottits) (protocol 9 - 11)

dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for a one-year-old female with breathing difficulties at --Address Redacted--.
Hello, I'm Zara with my partner Sam. We're here to help your daughter.
She's having trouble breathing. When did the problems begin?
She was fine this morning. Around noon she threw up and had diarrhea.
Let me assess her responsiveness. She's alert to pain and voice, but only occasionally to pain.
She opens her eyes sometimes, especially when touched.
Does she make sounds or try to respond verbally?
She cries when touched, and her arms and legs move vigorously when she's upset.
We'll assess her airway now. Can we look in her mouth to check for any blockage?
Airway is patent with a strong cry. No visible blockage.
She has rapid and labored breathing. I can see some retractions.
Her breaths are fast-around 60 per minute-with visible clavicular and abdominal retractions.
Inspiratory and expiratory wheezing with grunting is present.
Heavy wheezing and retraction-may indicate airway inflammation or blockage.
I'll check her pulse now. Let me feel her brachial artery.
Her heart rate is 160-very rapid with strong brachial pulse.
Pulse oximetry shows 78% oxygen saturation. She may need more oxygen.
No bleeding noticed, but her garment is wet with vomitus; diaper is dry.
Blood sugar is unobtainable with equipment we have.
Blood pressure not measurable-recorded as 0/0 in field notes.
Skin is hot, flushed, and she's diaphoretic.
May I look at her head and neck for injuries or issues?
Yes, proceed.
Head is normal shape. Pupils reactive but assessment is difficult due to crying and language.
Let me check her chest. Would you help calm her a bit?
Breathy wheezing heard and diminished breath sounds.
I'll check her belly next. Is that okay?
Yes. Belly is soft. She seems to have had some vomiting but nothing caught here.
She can move her arms and legs when she's crying and agitated.
Yes, she moves both when I hold her.
Skin is hot, diaphoretic, with some redness noted on cheeks.
I'll attach a 4-lead ECG for cardiac rhythm.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia now. Heart is in a consistent rhythm.
I'm also applying ETCO2 monitoring to track her ventilation and airway.
I'm following Protocol 9-11 for acute respiratory issues. We'll apply a non-rebreather oxygen mask.
High flow oxygen via non-rebreather at 20 LPM. Let me check to secure it.
She keeps the mask well. Let me help hold it.
She's in semi-Fowler's position to breathe more easily-will recheck in a moment.
She was vomiting and had diarrhea. How long has she been showing these symptoms?
Started this morning. Worse after noon. Then she began coughing and breathing harder.
She is one year old, first episode-correct? She's not on any medications or remedies?
Yes, first time sick. No medications or history of allergies.
What was her last meal or drink before symptoms?
She had rice soup and a bit of water at around 9 a.m.
Has she ever had difficulty breathing before?
No. She had no prior health issues at all.
Her birthday is in February, so she just turned one.
Yes, she turned exactly one. Her name is Sofia.
Can I get your address for the hospital?
We live on Dr. Martin Street.
Her GCS is 10, E2, V3, and M5. She's alert to touch and sound but not very cooperative.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you say she is in pain or discomfort?
She is very upset. I would say about a 7.
Rechecking her breathing, now 60 per minute. Let's check SpO2 again.
SpO2 has improved to 95%-significantly better. Good news.
Her heart rate has dropped to 150-still high, but slower now.
Rhythm is now sinus with 4-lead monitoring-we are seeing more normalcy.
The oxygen seems helping. Her breathing appears easier. Let's check again now.
We'll take her to [COMPANY_NAME] for pediatric assessment.
I think that is best for her.
We need to move her quickly. Safe to carry her in stretcher?
Yes, she is wrapped. I will help hold her during transfer.
We'll place her on stretcher and secure with ACR straps, and keep her in position.
Good plan. She is in semi-Fowler's for easier breathing during transport.
I agree with being at the same hospital with her.
Hospital has been called with pre-arrival report for the ER team at [COMPANY_NAME].
Central, en route with one-year-old Sofia from --Address Redacted-- to [COMPANY_NAME].
Initial SpO2 78% on arrival, 95% now. Vitals stable with sinus rhythm. Using NRB at 20LPM.
You can follow in your car to the hospital. She'll be safely moved in ambulance.
I'll follow.
Let's secure her now on the stretcher for transportation.
Let's continue monitoring EKG and ETCO2 while transporting.
She is receiving high-flow oxygen and is being repositioned as needed.
Confirming she is stable for transportation and in better condition for the trip.
Hospital ER is aware and ready to receive. All protocols followed.
She is now breathing more easily and secured for the ride.
We have her vitals updated through our field monitoring. All data sent ahead.
SpO2 last read at 96% with 56 respirations-moderate improvement.
She is lighter than a child her age, so I know her weight has declined.
Language is a barrier, translation was done by others here at the initial layer.
Sorry if I can't explain much due to my Spanish-language fluency only.
Thank you for helping. You were vital in keeping her calm and on the mask.
All systems are in place. Securely fastened and ready for the ambulance to move.
I'm holding the extra blanket and mask with her. She is with me the whole trip.
She was fully documented upon arrival, with EKG and capnography done on scene.
Oxygen is in place, and posture maintained for safe transport. All secured.
I'm so thankful for the fast care. She's in a great team.
Thank you for your trust in us. She's under critical care now.
All systems active-hospital ER aware of vitals. Let's roll.
I'm proud to see what you did here. You did make a difference.
You did too, her mother. She is in the best place for care now.
All transport and patient monitoring go smoothly from this point.
I'm very grateful for her fast transfer and exquisite care.
Hospital will be waiting with her team and updates constantly in hand.
Vitals will be updated on the fly with the hospital as we roll.
All items reviewed. EKG, capnography, and vitals are present. Ready to go.
She is now in your good care. She will meet the doctor.
Confirmed all monitoring in place. She is ready for the ambulance movement.
Let's confirm she is comfortable and secured before we take off.
I'm ready. Let's take her to the hospital.
